headline: Can Florida staff the economy it is trying to build?
summary: Talent Match connects one representative high-value occupation in each strategic cluster to the closest Florida public-university bachelor's pipeline, its first-year employment outcomes, and the named projects already creating delivery pressure.
coverageNote: This is a directional coverage proxy, not a labor-shortage forecast. Each pathway compares one representative occupation with one public-university bachelor's program. It excludes state-college, private, certificate, graduate, migration, promotion, and adjacent-occupation supply.
methodology: Demand uses Florida 2022-32 long-term occupational projections published through O*NET OnLine from Projections Central; openings include growth and replacement.
Pipeline outcomes use FETPIP's 2023-24 Florida public-university graduate report and the report's Florida employment matches.
The coverage proxy divides covered bachelor's graduates by projected annual openings. It sizes a visible pipeline; it does not label a shortage or surplus.
Institution leaders are the three largest disclosed producers in the selected FETPIP program; suppressed values remain unavailable.
Project pressure links only to named records in the Floridanomics project capex ledger and is not a complete hiring forecast.

sources.label: FETPIP 2023-24 graduate outcomes by program
sources.note: Florida public-university graduates, Florida employment matches, full-time employment, wages, and continuing education by CIP program.

clusters.label: AI & Software
clusters.question: Is Florida's computing pipeline scaling as fast as software demand?
clusters.read: The visible bachelor's pipeline covers less than one-third of projected Software Developer openings. That is not a shortage count, but it makes retention and adjacent credentials strategically important.

clusters.label: Aerospace & Space
clusters.question: Can the Space Coast convert a strong degree pipeline into project delivery?
clusters.read: Aerospace engineering output is roughly the scale of annual openings, but FETPIP finds only 41% of graduates in full-time Florida employment in the first follow-up window. Retention is the operating question.

clusters.label: Semiconductors & Electronics
clusters.question: Does Florida have the engineering bench for a larger semiconductor lane?
clusters.read: Electrical engineering output is broad relative to one representative occupation, but only 53% of graduates appear in full-time Florida employment. The next proof point is employer conversion, not simply more degrees.

clusters.label: Healthcare
clusters.question: Can Florida's nursing pipeline keep pace with a growing and aging state?
clusters.read: Registered Nursing has the largest covered pipeline and the strongest first-year placement, yet it reaches only one-fifth of projected annual RN openings. State-college and private-program supply matter here and are intentionally outside this first view.

clusters.label: Logistics & Supply Chain
clusters.question: Is Florida producing enough logistics talent to match its gateway advantage?
clusters.read: The covered logistics bachelor's pipeline is small against projected openings even as the occupation grows 31%. This is the clearest case for expanding the view into state colleges, certificates, and employer-linked credentials.

pipeline.program: Logistics, Materials and Supply Chain Management

clusters.label: Fintech & Finance
clusters.question: Can Florida turn a large finance pipeline into higher-value financial technology work?
clusters.read: Finance produces a large bachelor's class, but the first-year full-time Florida wage trails engineering pathways. The strategic move is specialization into analytics, risk, payments, and software-enabled finance.

clusters.label: Life Sciences
clusters.question: Can Florida convert biomedical graduates into an investable life-sciences cluster?
clusters.read: Biomedical engineering output is broad relative to one narrow occupation, but only 34% of graduates appear in full-time Florida employment. Competitor projects show why commercialization and employer density matter as much as degree volume.
